# Your grocery bill & the EU — research dossier (draft)

**Topic:** How would EU membership affect food prices / the grocery bill in Iceland?
**Chapter:** "Your grocery bill" (chapter 2)
**Status:** From one deep-research pass (23/25 claims confirmed, 2 killed). Auto-generated overnight; needs Gísli review + Icelandic write-up before becoming site content.
**Last updated:** 2026-06-17

> Confidence flags: ✅ verified (multi-vote) · ⚠️ partial/2-1 or time-sensitive · ❌ refuted (do not publish).

---

## BOTTOM LINE

**This is the chapter where membership changes the most.** Food and agriculture sit largely
*outside* the EEA — so unlike money, jobs, or most rules, EU membership would **materially change
Iceland's food regime.** Iceland today runs some of the **highest farm protection in the developed
world** (tariffs + quotas + support), which the OECD says directly inflates food prices. Membership
would replace that with the EU's common tariff + farm policy, most plausibly **lowering the price of
the currently-protected staples (dairy, meat, eggs)** — but not all of Iceland's high food cost is
tariffs (transport, small-market scale, wages also matter, and weren't quantified). The flip side:
domestic farming would face far more competition.

---

## TODAY (current Icelandic regime)
- ✅ **Food is the protected exception in a near-zero-tariff economy:** average applied agricultural
  tariff **~19.7–23.5%** vs **0.1%** on non-agricultural goods. (WTO Tariff Profile 2024; OECD 2025)
- ✅ **Highest duties on dairy & meat:** dairy avg **~73%** (max 182%, bound ceiling 574%); meat/live
  animals **~62–67%** (max 272%). Often compound duties (e.g. 30% + ISK 5–1,462/kg). (WTO; OECD)
- ✅ **Layered protection:** tariff-rate quotas on **~35%** of ag tariff lines, special safeguards on
  **~41.5%**. (WTO)
- ✅ **Targeted by design:** high duties fall on goods that compete with domestic production (dairy,
  meat, flowers, seasonal carrots/beets/potatoes); most other food — grains, pasta, rice, sugar, oils,
  fruit, year-round cucumbers/tomatoes/lettuce/peppers — is **already tariff-free** (62.7% of all
  tariff lines duty-free in 2007). So membership mainly hits the protected staples. (Bændasamtök
  Íslands; accession Chapter 11; WTO)
- ✅ **The protection shows up in the till:** OECD Producer Support Estimate **47%** of gross farm
  receipts (2nd-highest in OECD after Switzerland); farm-gate prices **~60% above** world levels;
  OECD calls it "production- and trade-distorting, contributing to inflated agricultural and food
  prices." Iceland had the **single highest dairy/eggs price level of 36 European countries in 2024**
  (index 172, +72% vs EU27). (OECD 2025; Eurostat 2024)

## AS A MEMBER (what changes)
- ✅ Iceland would join the **EU customs union + Common Customs Tariff**: food from EU states enters
  **without** today's high national tariffs; a common (often lower) external tariff applies to non-EU
  food. (European Commission / TARIC)
- ✅ Integration into the **Common Agricultural Policy** and common market organisations (dairy, meat,
  crops, etc.). CAP still protects EU borders, **but** reforms shifted the cost from consumers (price
  support) to **taxpayers** (direct payments, ~70% of CAP budget) — so EU food is cheaper to shoppers
  than Iceland's basket, though not world-priced. (USDA ERS 2025)
- ✅ Agriculture/food was **not aligned with the EU acquis** and is excluded from the EEA — hence the
  area of **biggest change.** (EUR-Lex / Commission COM(2011) 666, SWD(2012) 337)
- ✅ **Terms were never settled:** in the 2010–2013 talks the agriculture chapter **was never opened**;
  Iceland's negotiating position was unfinished when talks were suspended. (Icelandic govt summary)

## GAINS (for the grocery bill)
- ✅ **Cheaper protected staples** — dairy, meat, eggs are both the most-protected and most-expensive
  categories; removing the high tariffs is where prices could fall most. (OECD; Eurostat; TARIC)
- ✅ **Cost of farm support shifts from shopper to taxpayer** under CAP (direct payments vs
  consumer-borne price support) — you'd pay less at the till, more via taxes. (USDA ERS)

## LOSSES / COSTS
- ✅ **Domestic farming exposed to competition** — Iceland's govt and farming sector argue protection
  is necessary for production to survive harsh conditions; ⚠️ Bændasamtök (citing WTO) argue the EU
  itself permits broader ag protection (~70% of farm tariff lines vs Iceland's ~37%) — *partisan
  source + a quantity-vs-MFN terminology wrinkle, medium confidence.* (accession Ch.11; Bændasamtök)
- ✅ **Food security / rural livelihoods** concern if domestic dairy/meat/sheep production shrinks.
- ⚠️ **Fiscal cost:** CAP direct payments are taxpayer-funded — net household effect = grocery savings
  minus tax changes, **not quantified** by surviving evidence.

## UNCERTAIN / HONEST UNKNOWNS
- ⚠️ **No krónur estimate** of the per-household grocery-bill impact survived verification — an open
  gap worth a dedicated ASÍ/Hagstofa/price-survey search before publishing a number.
- ⚠️ **Decomposition not quantified:** how much of Iceland's high food price is tariffs vs transport
  vs small-market scale vs wages. Protection is a large, *measured* driver (60% farm-gate gap, 72%
  dairy premium) but its exact share isn't isolated.
- ⚠️ **Transition periods / derogations unknown** — terms never concluded. NOTE: the claim that "the
  EU grants no permanent derogations" was **❌ refuted (1-2)** — so permanent carve-outs are neither
  impossible nor guaranteed; don't assert either way.
- ⚠️ The accession-era **fresh-meat import ban** (flagged by the Commission) was later **struck down**
  by the EFTA Court (2016–17) — describes the past, not today.

## KEY SOURCES
**Primary:** OECD *Agricultural Policy Monitoring & Evaluation 2025 — Iceland*; WTO Tariff Profile
(Iceland) 2024; Eurostat comparative food price levels 2024; European Commission TARIC / CCT; USDA ERS
on CAP; EUR-Lex COM(2011) 666 & SWD(2012) 337; Icelandic govt accession Chapter 11 + Summary
Conclusions. **Farming side:** Bændasamtök Íslands (bondi.is — note URL now 404s, text mirrored on
bbl.is/rml.is). **Secondary:** Iceland Review, Neytendasamtökin (ns.is).

## TO FINISH

2. Get a **tariffs-vs-other-factors decomposition** of Iceland's food prices if one exists.
3. Re-verify the 2-1 farming-side comparison figures before quoting them.
